Chronic hyponatremia requiring two prior hospitalizations
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 24
History of childhood emotional neglect per prior psychiatric notes
No prior seizures or neurological diagnoses
family_history: Mother with generalized anxiety disorder and fibromyalgia
Maternal grandmother with epilepsy (diagnosed in her 50s)
No known family history of multiple sclerosis or movement disorders
Brother with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder

social_history.occupation: Elementary school teacher, currently on medical leave for 3 weeks
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any current or past use
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in apartment; recently separated from long-term partner 2 months ago

social_history.exercise: Previously ran 3-4 times per week; has not exercised in 6 weeks due to leg weakness and fatigue

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No dysarthria or word-finding difficulty. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Recall 3/3 at registration and 3/3 at 5 minutes. Attention intact; serial sevens performed accurately. Affect anxious with intermittent tearfulness, particularly when discussing recent stressors. MMSE 30/30.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus or ptosis. Facial sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial symmetry preserved at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in upper extremities; no rigidity or spasticity. In the left lower extremity, the patient demonstrates collapsing (give-way) weakness at hip flexion and knee extension, with initial effort appearing 4/5 that suddenly collapses to 1/5 when resistance is applied. Right lower extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. Positive Hoover sign on the left: when testing left hip flexion, involuntary extension is palpable in the contralateral right hip. Intermittent coarse tremor of the left hand noted during examination, variable in amplitude and frequency, which entrains to the frequency of voluntary rhythmic tapping with the right hand and resolves entirely when the patient is asked to perform rapid alternating movements with the right hand.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports complete loss of sensation to light touch, pinprick, temperature, and vibration in the left leg extending to the inguinal crease with an exact midline split at the abdomen. Proprioception intact bilaterally when tested. Right side sensation fully intact. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin on the right performed smoothly. On the left, the patient demonstrates inconsistent performance with large-amplitude wavering that does not follow a cerebellar pattern; performance improves with distraction.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient walks with a markedly antalgic pattern, dragging the left leg with circumduction. However, the pattern is inconsistent: when asked to walk backward, gait normalizes significantly. Tandem gait attempted with dramatic swaying but no falls. Romberg testing shows large-amplitude swaying in all directions that increases with observation but the patient consistently catches herself without falling.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a witnessed episode lasting approximately 2 minutes characterized by bilateral upper extremity tremor with asynchronous, irregular jerking, eyes tightly closed, and side-to-side head movements. The patient was verbally responsive during the episode, answering her name and following simple commands (e.g., 'squeeze my fingers'). The episode terminated abruptly without post-ictal confusion or drowsiness. Notably, serum sodium was checked and was 131 mEq/L at the time. The examiner observed that the patient's excessive water intake during the admission (estimated 5-6 liters per day) may be contributing to persistent mild hyponatremia.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive left leg weakness and numbness over 5 weeks, with three episodes of shaking and unresponsiveness over the past 2 weeks
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Reyes is a 32-year-old right-handed elementary school teacher who presents to the neurology inpatient service following transfer from an outside emergency department after an episode of bilateral shaking with apparent unresponsiveness witnessed by her coworker at school. She reports that the first such episode occurred approximately two weeks ago while she was at home alone. She describes suddenly feeling overwhelmed, followed by trembling in both arms that progressed to whole-body shaking lasting approximately one to two minutes. She states that she could hear her surroundings throughout the event but felt unable to move or speak. There was no tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or injury. She recovered rapidly without confusion. A second episode occurred one week later at a grocery store, witnessed by bystanders who called emergency services. She was taken to a local emergency department where a CT head was reported as normal, basic labs were drawn showing a serum sodium of 129 mEq/L, and she was discharged with a recommendation for outpatient neurology follow-up.

The third and most recent episode occurred yesterday at her workplace. A coworker described bilateral arm shaking and apparent unresponsiveness lasting approximately 90 seconds, during which the patient's eyes were tightly closed. The coworker reported that the patient seemed to respond to her name being called by squeezing her hand. No tonic posturing, cyanosis, or incontinence was noted. Emergency medical services were called and the patient was brought to the emergency department and subsequently admitted.

In addition to these episodic events, Ms. Reyes reports progressive weakness in her left leg that began approximately five weeks ago. She initially noticed difficulty going up stairs, and the weakness has worsened to the point where she now drags her left foot while walking. She also describes complete numbness of the left leg from the hip down, stating she cannot feel anything when she touches it. She denies any back pain, trauma, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or recent infections. She reports that the weakness fluctuates somewhat throughout the day and seems worse when she is under stress.

The patient has a notable medical history of psychogenic polydipsia diagnosed four years ago after evaluation for recurrent hyponatremia. She reports that she continues to drink large quantities of water daily, often 4 to 6 liters, describing an intense compulsion to drink that worsens with anxiety. Her most recent hospitalization for hyponatremia was eight months ago. She acknowledges significant recent psychosocial stressors, including separation from her partner of six years two months ago and increasing workplace demands that have left her feeling overwhelmed. She has been on medical leave for three weeks due to her neurological symptoms. Her grandmother had epilepsy, which has heightened her fear that she may also have epilepsy. She has been reading about seizure disorders online and is convinced she has developed the same condition. She reports poor sleep with frequent awakenings, decreased appetite, and a 4 kg weight loss over the past two months.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Symmetric without focal slowing or asymmetry
background.sleep_features: Sleep spindles and vertex waves present; normal sleep architecture observed

findings.frequency: One event captured during the 3-hour recording
findings.morphology: During a witnessed clinical event consisting of bilateral asynchronous upper extremity tremor with eyes tightly closed and side-to-side head movements lasting 110 seconds, the EEG demonstrated continuous normal background activity with preserved posterior dominant rhythm. No ictal EEG correlate was identified. No rhythmic or periodic discharges. No post-event slowing or suppression.

artifacts.description: Prominent muscle and movement artifact during the clinical event, obscuring frontal and temporal channels intermittently. Posterior channels maintained adequate quality throughout, allowing confident interpretation.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: 3 minutes of hyperventilation performed. Patient reported lightheadedness and tingling in fingers. No electrographic abnormality elicited. No clinical event provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Photic stimulation at 1-30 Hz performed. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal EEG with one clinical event captured. No epileptiform discharges identified. Normal background activity throughout. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during the clinical event partially obscured frontal and temporal channels, though posterior channels remained interpretable. Routine recording duration limits detection of rare interictal discharges.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality identified. No white matter lesions. No periventricular or juxtacortical hyperintensities.

additional_observations: Hippocampi symmetric in size and signal bilaterally
Normal appearance of the basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem
Normal pituitary gland size and signal — no mass or stalk thickening
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
Incidental left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening, likely inflammatory or allergic
mri.impression: Normal MRI of the brain without and with contrast. No evidence of structural lesion, demyelination, stroke, or mass. Normal pituitary gland. Incidental left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening of no clinical significance. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No evidence of chamber enlargement or hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia detected.

Additional.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 25 minutes after witnessed episode)

findings.type: Clinical event 1 — bilateral tremor with apparent unresponsiveness

EEG showed continuous normal awake background with muscle artifact. No ictal EEG correlate. Posterior dominant rhythm maintained throughout.

findings.type: Clinical event 2 — left leg weakness episode

findings.type: Clinical event 3 — whole-body shaking with emotional trigger

Patient responded to verbal commands during the event (opened eyes briefly when asked). EEG showed persistent normal background without any ictal pattern. No post-event slowing.

findings.type: Clinical event 4 — brief tremor episode during sleep-wake transition
findings.morphology: Brief bilateral upper extremity tremor lasting 25 seconds while patient was lying in bed. Patient appeared awake and oriented immediately before and after. EEG showed normal wake background with no epileptiform correlate.

artifacts.description: Significant movement artifact during clinical events, particularly affecting frontal and temporal channels. Posterior channels maintained adequate quality for interpretation throughout all events.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed on day 1; no electrographic abnormalities. Patient reported tingling but no clinical event.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving; no photoparoxysmal response.
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Relative sleep deprivation on night 2; no interictal epileptiform discharges identified.

output.impression: Normal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. No interictal epileptiform discharges identified throughout the recording. Normal background activity with preserved sleep architecture. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during events partially obscured some channels, but posterior channels maintained sufficient quality for confident interpretation.

output.query: functional neurological disorder psychogenic polydipsia comorbid somatoform

results.title: Psychogenic polydipsia and functional neurological disorder: shared mechanisms of somatization
results.journal: Journal of Neurology Neurosurgery and Psychiatry
results.summary: Both psychogenic polydipsia and FND can be conceptualized within a framework of altered interoceptive processing, where internal bodily signals are misinterpreted or amplified. Shared psychiatric comorbidities (anxiety, depression, trauma history) are common.

results.title: Diagnosis and management of functional neurological disorder
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A et al.
results.summary: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, etc.), not by exclusion. A multidisciplinary approach combining neurology, psychiatry, physiotherapy, and psychology is recommended.

results.title: Hyponatremia and seizures: distinguishing organic from functional etiologies
results.summary: Hyponatremia below 120 mEq/L can provoke true epileptic seizures. Mild hyponatremia (130-135 mEq/L) is unlikely to cause seizures in isolation but may lower seizure threshold. In patients with PNES, coexisting mild hyponatremia should not lead to misdiagnosis of epileptic seizures.

output.summary: Psychogenic polydipsia and FND share underlying mechanisms related to altered interoceptive processing and somatization, and frequently co-occur with anxiety, depression, and trauma history. Mild hyponatremia from polydipsia (>125 mEq/L) is unlikely to cause true seizures but represents a diagnostic confounder. FND diagnosis relies on positive clinical signs, not exclusion. Treatment involves integrated psychiatric and neurological management.

output.proposed: Sertraline 100 mg daily with current clinical scenario (hyponatremia, polydipsia)
interactions: SSRIs including sertraline can cause or exacerbate hyponatremia via SIADH, particularly in the first weeks of treatment and in patients over 65 or with low body weight. In this patient on chronic stable-dose sertraline, SIADH is a less likely primary etiology given the maximally dilute urine osmolality (which argues against SIADH), but the medication may have a contributory effect.
warnings: Monitor sodium levels during SSRI therapy, particularly in patients with known polydipsia and hyponatremia
If switching to an SNRI (e.g., duloxetine, venlafaxine), hyponatremia risk persists with all serotonergic agents
Desmopressin concurrent use with SSRI increases risk of hyponatremia — exercise caution
alternatives: Bupropion (lower risk of SIADH, but less effective for anxiety)
CBT as non-pharmacological alternative for anxiety management

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Baseline serum sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Baseline serum osmolality 268 mOsm/kg (L), Baseline urine osmolality 62 mOsm/kg (L), 24-hour urine volume (pre-restriction) 6200 mL (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Baseline urine osmolality: 62 mOsm/kg — maximally dilute (H)
24-hour urine volume: 6200 mL — polyuria from excess intake (H)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with mixed symptoms — psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional limb weakness, comorbid with psychogenic polydipsia

differential.key_features: Mild hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) from psychogenic polydipsia could theoretically lower seizure threshold. However, sodium >125 mEq/L rarely causes seizures. Witnessed events have no EEG correlate, post-event prolactin is normal, and semiology (asynchronous movements, eyes tightly closed, preserved responsiveness) is inconsistent with epileptic seizures. This is the key diagnostic confounder in this case.

Family history of epilepsy (grandmother) raises concern. However, EEG captured events without correlate, events have features characteristic of PNES (asynchronous movements, tight eye closure, preserved awareness, lack of post-ictal confusion), and post-event prolactin is normal.

Progressive unilateral weakness and sensory changes in a young woman could suggest MS. However, brain MRI shows no white matter lesions, spine MRI is normal, and clinical signs are positive for functional etiology (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, midline sensory split).

Unilateral weakness could suggest vascular etiology, but gradual onset over weeks (not acute), normal MRI with no DWI restriction, and positive functional signs argue strongly against. Patient's age and lack of vascular risk factors also make this unlikely.

Unilateral weakness and sensory loss could suggest peripheral nerve pathology, but the non-dermatomal distribution, midline sensory split, normal reflexes, and normal EMG/NCS all argue against this diagnosis.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, distractibility, give-way weakness, midline splitting of sensory loss)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, entrainable tremor, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split — all positive indicators of functional neurological disorder

optimal_actions.action: Order EEG with attempt to capture a habitual event
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG; captured event shows no electrographic correlate, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to rule out structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with no evidence of demyelination, mass, or stroke

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, serum osmolality, urine osmolality, thyroid function, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia with dilute urine consistent with psychogenic polydipsia; normal post-event prolactin arguing against epileptic seizure

optimal_actions.action: Recognize mild hyponatremia as a confounding factor and evaluate its contribution — determine that it is due to psychogenic polydipsia (not SIADH) and that sodium level is too mild to cause seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Low serum osmolality with maximally dilute urine indicates appropriate renal response to excess water intake, excluding SIADH. Sodium 131 mEq/L is above seizure threshold (~120 mEq/L).

optimal_actions.action: Order prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture multiple events and confirm PNES
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple events captured without EEG correlate, confirming PNES diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider EMG/NCS of the affected lower extremity to rule out peripheral nerve pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal motor and sensory nerve conduction; normal EMG

optimal_actions.action: Consider spine MRI to rule out myelopathy given progressive leg weakness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cervical and thoracic spine MRI

optimal_actions.action: Refer for psychiatric evaluation to assess comorbid conditions and relationship between FND and psychogenic polydipsia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of comorbid anxiety, adjustment disorder, and recognition that polydipsia and FND share underlying somatization mechanisms

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis transparently using positive clinical signs, refer to CBT and specialized physiotherapy, and coordinate management of both FND and polydipsia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding and engagement with multidisciplinary treatment plan

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, midline sensory split) rather than diagnosing by exclusion alone
Obtain EEG (ideally video-EEG) demonstrating absence of ictal correlate during a captured clinical event
Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
Check post-event prolactin to help differentiate PNES from epileptic seizures
Recognize that hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) from psychogenic polydipsia is a confounding finding but is too mild to cause seizures and does not explain the focal neurological signs
Communicate the diagnosis supportively using positive language and evidence from examination
Refer for psychiatric evaluation and evidence-based treatment (CBT, specialized physiotherapy)
contraindicated_actions: Starting anti-epileptic drugs for presumed epileptic seizures without EEG confirmation — AEDs are not effective for PNES and expose the patient to unnecessary side effects
Attributing the seizure-like episodes solely to hyponatremia and treating only the sodium without addressing FND — the semiology and EEG findings clearly indicate PNES, not hyponatremic seizures
Aggressively correcting sodium with hypertonic saline — mild chronic hyponatremia from polydipsia does not require urgent correction, and rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome
Dismissing symptoms as 'not real' or 'all in the patient's head' — this damages therapeutic alliance and worsens outcomes
Prescribing benzodiazepines as seizure abortive therapy for witnessed PNES events
Ordering excessive invasive procedures (brain biopsy, repeated lumbar punctures) when clinical signs positively support FND
Discontinuing sertraline abruptly — while SSRIs can contribute to hyponatremia, abrupt discontinuation may worsen anxiety and functional symptoms
key_reasoning_points: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split), not merely by exclusion of other diagnoses
The dissociation between clinical seizure-like events and normal EEG activity is the hallmark of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES)

The coexistence of psychogenic polydipsia and FND illustrates a pattern of somatization — psychological distress manifesting through bodily symptoms and behaviors — which strengthens the FND diagnosis

Mild hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) is a key diagnostic confounder: it could theoretically be invoked as a seizure etiology, but the sodium level is above the seizure threshold (~120 mEq/L), the event semiology is inconsistent with epileptic seizures, and the EEG shows no correlate

The maximally dilute urine osmolality (62 mOsm/kg) with low serum osmolality excludes SIADH and confirms the hyponatremia is due to excess free water intake (psychogenic polydipsia), not an endocrine or renal etiology
Normal post-event prolactin supports PNES over epileptic seizures for convulsive events
Family history of epilepsy (grandmother) is a red herring that may heighten the agent's index of suspicion for epilepsy, but clinical and electrophysiological findings definitively point to PNES
Treatment requires integrated management addressing both FND (CBT, physiotherapy) and psychogenic polydipsia (behavioral water restriction, anxiety management) through a multidisciplinary approach

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to the diagnostic confounder of coexisting psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyponatremia, which could be misattributed as the cause of seizure-like episodes. Family history of epilepsy adds further misdirection. The agent must correctly identify that the hyponatremia is too mild to cause seizures and recognize the positive functional signs pointing to FND/PNES.
clinical_pearls: Psychogenic polydipsia and FND share underlying mechanisms of somatization and altered interoceptive processing
Hyponatremia below 120 mEq/L can cause seizures; levels of 130-135 mEq/L are unlikely to provoke epileptic activity
Maximally dilute urine in the setting of hyponatremia indicates appropriate renal response to excess water, confirming primary polydipsia and excluding SIADH
Hoover sign: involuntary hip extension on the contralateral side when testing hip flexion — a reliable positive sign of functional weakness
Tremor entrainment: functional tremor changes frequency to match voluntary rhythmic movements of another limb
Tight eye closure during seizure-like events is more common in PNES than epileptic seizures
Midline splitting of sensory loss is a positive functional sign, as true spinal or peripheral lesions do not produce exact midline split
